name: Sidhe Mounds
description: Sidhe Mounds, also known as fairy mounds or sidhe hills, are prominent features in Irish mythology and folklore. They are believed to be the dwelling places of the Aos Sí, the supernatural race often equated with fairies, gods, or spirits of the dead. These mounds are not merely geographical formations; they are portals to the Otherworld, a realm of magic, enchantment, and timelessness. Entering a sidhe mound could lead to encounters with powerful beings, grand feasts, or perilous quests. Time flows differently within, and mortals who linger too long may find years have passed in their own world upon their return. The mounds are often associated with ancient burial sites or ringforts, suggesting a connection to ancestor veneration and the spirits of the land. Respect for these sacred places is paramount, as disturbing them is said to bring misfortune or the wrath of the Aos Sí. Stories abound of musicians, warriors, and even kings who entered the mounds and returned transformed, blessed, or cursed. The sidhe mounds represent a liminal space between the mortal and supernatural realms, a constant reminder of the magical forces that permeate the Irish landscape and imagination. They are places of both wonder and danger, embodying the enduring power of the Otherworld in Irish culture.
subtitle: Gateways to the Otherworld

keyMyths.title: Oisín in Tír na nÓg
keyMyths.description: Oisín, a legendary warrior, is lured to Tír na nÓg (Land of Youth) by Niamh Chinn Óir. He spends what he believes is a short time there, only to return to Ireland and find centuries have passed.

keyMyths.title: Knockfierna
keyMyths.description: The hill of Knockfierna in County Limerick is said to be the home of Áine, a powerful fairy queen. Stories tell of mortals being taken into the sidhe mound and experiencing both blessings and curses.

sources.author: Lady Gregory
sources.description: A collection of Irish myths and legends.
sources.text: A collection of Irish myths and legends.
